title: JTBD Analysis — Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions

sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape
sections.content:
### 1.1 CFJ, Output Types, and Offering Level

| NAICS | CFJ | Output Type | Offering Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 712110 | Acquire, preserve, and present cultural, historical, natural, and scientific heritage to the public through curated exhibitions, conservation, and educational programming | Art museum | L7 (Ecosystem) |
| | | Natural history / science museum | L7 |
| | | History museum | L7 |
| | | Archaeological site / open-air museum | L7 |
| | | Science & technology center | L7 |

**Routing:** L7 Ecosystem → service NAICS. Phases 1.4 (Product BOM) and 1.5 (Edges) are skipped — no physical manufactured output; service composition applies. Emergency exit monitoring activates universally across all five output types (L6H-SEC is fully universal per VN activation matrix).

**Scale context:** ~104,000 museums worldwide (UNESCO estimate); ~33,000 in the US, ~27,000 in Europe. [Source: [Statista Museums Worldwide](https://www.statista.com/statistics/1201800/number-of-museums-worldwide-by-region/)] Heritage buildings — those subject to listed building or monument protection — constitute a significant proportion of the European stock and disproportionately rely on non-invasive, battery-powered security solutions because cable routing through historic fabric requires planning authority consent. [Source: [Historic England Emergency Lighting Guidance](https://historicengland.org.uk/advice/technical-advice/building-services-engineering/internal-external-and-emergency-lighting/)]

### 1.2 Segments

Circumscribed by **operational context** (different alternatives and trade-offs), not demographics or institution size.

| # | Segment | Defining Circumstance | Why distinct |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | Large art / natural history museum | >200K visitors/yr; professional security team; high-value portable collection; BMS likely present | Primary theft prevention concern; highest consequence per undetected exit event; IoT dashboard replaces costly guard post |
| S2 | Historic house / heritage site (listed building) | Protected structure; cannot route wiring without heritage consent; minimal permanent staff | C5 (surface mount) is an **advantage**, not a friction; battery-powered wireless is the only viable retrofit option |
| S3 | Regional / community museum | Small paid staff + volunteers; limited technical capacity; tight budget | IoT price premium (C7) is a barrier; ROI must be simple and documentable; commissioning complexity is a decision killer |
| S4 | Archaeological / outdoor site | Dispersed perimeter; intermittent public access seasons; remote or semi-remote locations | Range constraint (C3) is primary; battery life (C4) critical where maintenance visits are infrequent; no BMS |
| S5 | Multi-site museum group / national body | Manages portfolio of buildings under one security governance structure | Cross-site IoT dashboard is the decisive value proposition; protocol lock-in (C6) relevant if centralized BMS is in scope |

**Anti-pattern avoided:** "Well-funded vs. underfunded museum" is demographics. S1–S5 are circumstance-defined.

### 1.3 Process Value Network

Full VN documented in `VN_museums_historical_sites.md`. Key nodes:

| L6 Node | Job Family | Türwächter Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| L6H-SEC Building Security & Life Safety | Protect collections, staff, and visitors against theft, unauthorized access, fire, and emergency exit misuse at all times | **DIRECT ANCHOR** — product enters at L5 Emergency Exit Security Devices |
| L6H-FM Facility Management & Maintenance | Maintain building fabric, utilities, and physical infrastructure in operational and compliant condition | Adjacent — heritage maintenance constraints amplify non-invasive installation requirement |
| L6b Conservation & Collections Care | Stabilize, treat, and store collection objects to prevent deterioration | Adjacent — conservation storage areas are highest-risk exit points (direct collection access) |

---

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Anchor unit** | Emergency Exit Security Devices |
| **Anchor level** | L5 (standalone product/system) |
| **Parent VN unit** | Building Security & Life Safety (L6H-SEC) |
| **Parent level** | L6 (horizontal process) |
| **Anchor type** | PROCESS_VN — product is used by the museum operator; it does not become part of the cultural output |
| **Alternative slot** | Alarmed exit hardware with IoT monitoring (~20% market share in museum sector) |
| **Constraint profile** | C5 (surface mount) = **advantage** in heritage buildings; C3 (range) = primary risk in large sites; C4 (battery) = critical at low-maintenance sites; C8 (GDPR) = EU public buildings; C6 (EasyWave protocol lock-in) = relevant for S1/S5 with BMS |

### 2.2 Lens

**Function verb** from L6H-SEC job_family: *protect*

> "What must museums be able to **protect** their collection areas and escape routes against — in the context of Building Security & Life Safety — while maintaining fire-code-compliant emergency egress in buildings where structural modification is restricted?"

Segments filtered to: S1, S2, S5 (primary); S3 (secondary); S4 (adjacent — product fit is weaker at remote outdoor sites due to range).

### 2.3 Core Job and Product Job

| Level | Type | Statement |
|---|---|---|
| L6H-SEC parent | Core Functional Job (CFJ) | Acquire, preserve, and present cultural, historical, natural, and scientific heritage to the public through curated exhibitions, conservation, and educational programming |
| L5 anchor | **Product Job** | Help Building Security & Life Safety to detect and deter unauthorized use of emergency exit doors in collection spaces and storage areas while maintaining fire-code-compliant escape capability in heritage buildings where structural modification is prohibited or restricted |

**Abstraction test:** 3+ alternatives from different categories exist (mechanical alarm, wired intruder detection, gallery attendants, CCTV, do nothing) → confirmed Core Job at this level.

**Museum-specific note:** In this market the product job carries a dual security dimension absent in hospitality — unauthorized exit activation in a collection area is treated as a **potential theft attempt**, not merely a fire-code nuisance event. This elevates Importance scores on detection needs relative to the hotels market.

### 2.4 Alternatives

| # | Alternative | UNSPSC | Inherent Trade-offs |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | Basic mechanical door alarm (no monitoring) — 45% share | 46171610 | No remote visibility; requires physical patrol to detect unreported activation; no audit log; no battery required for alarm |
| A2 | Wired intruder detection + door contacts (alarm panel) — 25% share | 46171601 | Wiring through heritage fabric requires planning consent and conservation officer approval — major barrier in listed buildings; high retrofit cost |
| A3 | **Alarmed exit hardware with IoT monitoring** (Türwächter) — 20% share | 46171601 | Proprietary wireless protocol (C6); battery dependency (C4); ~730 EUR/door (C7); surface-mount profile is **accepted** in heritage context |
| A4 | Gallery attendant / security guard patrol | 92121500 | High recurring labor cost (security = ~22% of museum operating budget); [Source: [Museum Running Costs](https://financialmodelslab.com/blogs/operating-costs/museum)] coverage gaps when attendant is elsewhere; no event log; scales linearly with door count |
| A5 | No dedicated exit monitoring (do nothing) — 10% share | — | Zero installation burden; zero P1 burden; compliance risk is distributed across insurance, fire marshal, and grant-funding conditions |

**"Do nothing" is A5** — zero trade-offs, zero P1 burden; explains its persistence in underfunded regional museums despite clear compliance and theft-detection gaps.

### 2.5 Scenarios and Output Type Activation

Output type codes: **ART** | **NAT** | **HIST** | **ARCH** | **SCI**

| # | Scenario | Output types | Alternatives in play |
|---|---|---|---|
| SC1 | After-hours unauthorized activation of exit from collections storage vault or conservation area (possible theft attempt) | ART, NAT, HIST | A3, A4 |
| SC2 | Visitor pushing through "emergency exit only" gallery door during opening hours (access to restricted area or shortcut) | ART, NAT, HIST, SCI | A1, A3, A4 |
| SC3 | Contractor/maintenance worker propping exit door during infrastructure work in a gallery or plant room | ART, NAT, HIST, SCI | A1, A3, A4 |
| SC4 | Multi-site museum group: centralized security monitoring across geographically distributed buildings with minimal staff at each | ART, HIST | A3 (IoT only) |
| SC5 | Heritage authority / fire marshal / insurer inspection — documentation of exit monitoring compliance required | All | A1, A2, A3 (documentation requirement) |
| SC6 | Historic building retrofit: wired security system is impractical; battery-powered surface-mount device is the only consented option | HIST, ART | A1 vs. A3 |

**Sparsity check:** SC4 only activates ART/HIST (not ARCH/SCI at portfolio level) → Core Job granular enough; no split required.

---

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### Stakeholder Roles

| Role | Person in 712110 | Pyramid levels | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Job Executor | Security officer / gallery attendant (monitoring duty) | P1 (all), P2, P3, P4, P5 | Monitors dashboard, responds to activations, investigates incidents |
| Job Overseer | Head of Security / Museum Director | P1 (own), P2, P4 | Accountable for theft prevention, compliance KPIs, insurer requirements |
| Job Beneficiary | Visitors and staff | P2, P5 | Safe evacuation; unimpeded escape |
| Lifecycle Support — Specifier | Facilities manager / conservation engineer | P1 Acquisition | Defines specification; must obtain heritage consent for any structural work |
| Lifecycle Support — Installer | Security integrator / maintenance technician | P1 Preparation | Surface-mount installation; gateway pairing; heritage-compliant fixing |
| Lifecycle Support — Validator | Fire marshal / heritage conservation officer | P1 Confirm | Signs off on fire safety compliance and conservation reversibility |
| Lifecycle Support — Maintainer | Facilities team / security officer | P1 Maintenance | Battery replacement; signal level checks; remote dashboard health |
| Purchase Executor | Head of Finance / Facilities Director | P1 Acquisition | CAPEX approval; grant-funding eligibility check |
| Purchase Influencer | Insurer; heritage funder (Arts Council, Historic England); fire authority | P2, P4 | Sets compliance and reversibility requirements; may co-fund if aligned with heritage protection goals |

### 3.1 Burleson Pyramid — P1 Product Jobs per Alternative

| Stage | A1 Basic mech. alarm | A2 Wired alarm panel | A3 IoT monitoring (Türwächter) | A4 Guard / attendant | A5 Do nothing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acquire** | Specify per-door unit; quote; order | Engage integrator; heritage consent application; wiring survey; planning permission | Specify device+gateway; RF coverage survey; assess battery life for door frequency; certify conservation reversibility | Issue RFP or hire in-house; schedule SIA-licensed staff; SLA negotiation | — |
| **Prepare** | Surface-mount per door; key provisioning; no wiring | Obtain listed building consent; route conduit; install panel; programme zones | Surface-mount device; pair to gateway; configure zones; test alarm + radio; verify no permanent fixings required by heritage officer | Define patrol schedules and coverage map; brief staff; configure incident log | — |
| **Use** | Manual patrol to verify no propping; respond only if alarm is heard | Alarm panel event response; physical investigation per alert | Dashboard monitoring; alert triage by priority zone (storage vault vs. public gallery); dispatch investigation | Attendant presence in gallery; rounds outside hours; telephone incident report | Accept coverage gap; rely on CCTV retrospectively |
| **Maintain** | Periodic tamper check; no battery required | Annual panel service; wiring inspection; heritage-impacting maintenance needs re-consent | Battery replacement (~every 2 yrs); firmware update; signal level check; conservation environment check (temperature/humidity impact on battery) | Staff turnover; re-training; rota management; fatigue and coverage gap management | — |
| **Dispose** | Remove hardware; fill screw holes in historic masonry | Remove hardware; remove conduit; make-good — heritage restoration required | Remove device; decommission from dashboard; no permanent mark on historic fabric | Contract termination; knowledge transfer | — |

**Key insight:** A2 (Wired) has the highest Acquisition/Preparation burden in museum context — heritage consent is a project-in-itself that can take 3–12 months. A3 (Türwächter) is the only alternative that is both **remotely monitored** and **reversible without heritage damage**, making it structurally differentiated in listed-building segments. A5 has zero P1 burden — explains persistence in S3 budget museums.

### 3.2 Job Map — 8 Universal Steps

| # | Step | Statement (museum context) | Relevant to product |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DEFINE | Determine which emergency exits require monitoring (storage vaults, conservation labs, galleries, public exits), alert priority by zone, and notification escalation rules | YES — zone configuration is critical; vault exits > public gallery exits |
| 2 | LOCATE | Map all emergency exit points across building; assess wireless signal paths through historic masonry; identify gateway placement compatible with heritage constraints | YES — RF penetration through thick stone/brick walls is a key variable |
| 3 | PREPARE | Install monitoring devices on door handles without drilling into historic fabric; pair to gateway; configure zone priorities; test alarm tone audibility | YES — surface-mount, no-drill installation is the primary market differentiator |
| 4 | CONFIRM | Verify every device transmits through heritage-grade masonry walls, alarm is unobstructed, escape motion is unrestricted, and heritage officer confirms reversibility | YES — acceptance testing must include RF validation through actual wall materials |
| 5 | EXECUTE | Maintain all monitored exits in secured state 24/7; trigger alert on unauthorized activation; distinguish high-risk zones (storage) from lower-risk zones (public exits) | YES — core function; after-hours monitoring is the primary use case |
| 6 | MONITOR | Track real-time exit status from centralized dashboard; receive alerts prioritized by zone risk level; correlate with CCTV on high-risk events | YES — primary IoT value; after-hours dashboard access on mobile is essential for lean security teams |
| 7 | MODIFY | Respond to and reset triggered devices; adjust zone alert rules; replace failed units with minimal disruption to gallery operations or open days | YES — operational; reset must not require tool or key unavailable on night shift |
| 8 | CONCLUDE | Generate compliance audit reports for fire marshal, insurer, and heritage authority; proactively replace batteries before monitoring capability is lost | YES — compliance documentation is a grant-funding and insurance requirement for many institutions |

### 3.3 Error Statements — P1 + P2 (Seven Error Types)

| # | Full Statement | Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | OS | Impact | p_related | Confidence | Underserved |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1 | Minimize the likelihood of high-value collection storage area exits remaining unmonitored due to incomplete upfront coverage planning, leaving ≥1 after-hours exit event undetected per quarter | DEFINE | DETECTION | 8 | 4 | 12 | safety | false | 0.80 | ✓ |
| E2 | Increase the ability to segment emergency exit monitoring alerts by zone risk level (storage vault vs. public gallery vs. service corridor) so that a single after-hours activation of a vault exit triggers immediate escalation | DEFINE | ACCURACY | 8 | 3 | 13 | safety | false | 0.75 | ✓ |
| E3 | Increase the ability to pre-validate wireless signal coverage through heritage-grade masonry walls (stone, brick, reinforced concrete) before committing to gateway placement and device purchase | LOCATE | ACCURACY | 7 | 4 | 10 | reliability | **true** | 0.75 | — |
| E4 | Minimize the likelihood of RF signal loss between exit devices and the gateway caused by thick historic masonry walls, creating silent monitoring gaps exceeding 30 minutes | LOCATE | ROBUSTNESS | 8 | 5 | 11 | reliability | **true** | 0.70 | — |
| E5 | Minimize the time required to install and commission emergency exit monitoring across a 20-room museum without drilling into historic fabric — achieving full coverage in <1 day | PREPARE | EFFORT | 8 | 5 | 11 | time | **true** | 0.80 | — |
| E6 | Minimize the likelihood of installation fixing methods leaving permanent marks or penetrations in listed building fabric, causing non-compliance with heritage conservation officer requirements | PREPARE | ROBUSTNESS | 9 | 6 | 12 | safety | false | 0.85 | — |
| E7 | Increase the ability to verify that every installed exit device transmits correctly through actual building materials and that alarm operation is unimpeded — within a single commissioning walk-through that the heritage officer can witness | CONFIRM | ACCURACY | 8 | 5 | 11 | reliability | false | 0.75 | — |
| E8 | Minimize the likelihood of an unauthorized emergency exit activation in a storage vault or conservation area remaining undetected for >2 minutes during closed hours | EXECUTE | DETECTION | 10 | 3 | 17 | safety | false | 0.90 | ✓ |
| E9 | Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit monitoring device adding any resistance or additional step to the escape path that could delay evacuation of visitors with reduced mobility | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | 10 | 8 | 12 | safety | false | 0.90 | — |
| E10 | Minimize the likelihood of legitimate authorized uses (staff exiting at end of shift, conservation team accessing storage) generating false alerts that erode duty manager confidence in the monitoring system | EXECUTE | ACCURACY | 7 | 4 | 10 | reliability | false | 0.75 | — |
| E11 | Increase the ability of a single duty security officer or remote monitoring team to track real-time exit status across all monitored doors from one centralized interface without physical patrol — during closed-hours coverage windows | MONITOR | THROUGHPUT | 9 | 3 | 15 | time | false | 0.85 | ✓ |
| E12 | Minimize the time from emergency exit activation event to security officer notification to <60 seconds during after-hours monitoring periods | MONITOR | DETECTION | 9 | 4 | 14 | time | false | 0.85 | ✓ |
| E13 | Minimize the likelihood of wireless connectivity loss to any monitored exit device creating a silent gap exceeding 15 minutes — particularly during after-hours periods when no staff are on-site | MONITOR | ROBUSTNESS | 8 | 5 | 11 | reliability | **true** | 0.75 | — |
| E14 | Minimize the time to reset a triggered exit monitoring device and restore it to active monitoring state after an incident investigation — without requiring specialist tools not available to on-duty museum staff | MODIFY | EFFORT | 7 | 6 | 8 | time | false | 0.70 | — |
| E15 | Minimize the likelihood of a high-priority storage area activation being deprioritized due to alert fatigue from higher-frequency low-risk public gallery exit events | MODIFY | DETECTION | 8 | 5 | 11 | safety | false | 0.75 | — |
| E16 | Increase the ability to produce a complete, timestamped digital log of all emergency exit activations and response actions formatted for submission to the fire authority, insurer, and heritage funder — without manual compilation | CONCLUDE | EFFORT | 8 | 3 | 13 | safety | false | 0.80 | ✓ |
| E17 | Minimize the time required to identify and proactively replace batteries approaching depletion threshold across a deployment of ≥10 exit monitoring devices before monitoring capability is lost — accounting for reduced battery performance in climate-controlled conservation environments | CONCLUDE | DETECTION | 7 | 4 | 10 | reliability | **true** | 0.75 | — |
| E18 | Minimize the variability in emergency exit monitoring coverage status across the building at any time — ensuring no exits enter an unmonitored state due to technical failure without triggering a staff notification | P2 Core | CONSISTENCY | 9 | 4 | 14 | safety | false | 0.85 | ✓ |
| E19 | Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit compliance deficiencies being cited during fire marshal inspection or heritage authority review — resulting in temporary closure of gallery spaces or loss of grant-funding eligibility | P2 Core | ACCURACY | 9 | 5 | 13 | safety | false | 0.80 | — |

**Count:** 19 statements. Product-related=true: E3, E4, E5, E13, E17 = **5/19 = 26%**. Market-inherent: **14/19 = 74%**.

**Underserved (imp > 7 AND sat < 5):** E1, E2, E8, E11, E12, E16, E18 — 7 needs.

### 3.4 P3–P5 Bridge Questions

| Level | Role | Retrieval | Bridge | Market Need |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P3 Role | Security officer / duty manager (executor) | "Becomes the person who prevented the theft — or who can prove it was investigated within minutes" | "What must the system do so they CAN demonstrate rapid, documented response to any after-hours exit event?" | Increase the ability to demonstrate evidence-based, timestamped response to after-hours exit activations — to insurers and collections trustees — without manual log reconstruction [hypothesis] |
| P4 Image | Museum Director / Head of Security (overseer) | "The board, insurers, and heritage funders must see: this institution takes collection protection seriously and uses state-of-the-art monitoring compatible with its building's heritage status" | "What must the system do so funders and auditors SEE a professional, non-invasive protection approach?" | Minimize the likelihood of a preventable exit monitoring failure becoming a documented finding in an insurer audit or heritage authority inspection that affects funding eligibility [hypothesis] |
| P5 Emotional | Duty security officer (executor, overnight) | "Wants to feel they would know immediately if someone was in the building after hours; fears being the person who didn't notice until a piece went missing" | "What must the system do to produce confidence in continuous after-hours coverage with minimal staff?" | Minimize the sense of uncertainty about whether high-risk storage and collection exits are truly secured during closed-hours periods when no patrol is possible [hypothesis] |

### 3.5 ODI Score Summary

| Rank | Error Statement (short) | OS | Segment priority | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | E8 — Vault exit activation undetected >2 min after hours | **17** | S1, S2, S5 | Core differentiation against A1 and A4 |
| 2 | E11 — Single officer covers all exits, no patrol | **15** | S1, S5 | IoT dashboard is the primary labour-cost ROI story |
| 3 | E12 — Alert latency <60 sec after hours | **14** | S1, S2, S5 | Network reliability proof point |
| 4 | E18 — Monitoring consistency, no silent gaps | **14** | S1, S2, S5 | Always-on vs. mechanical-only incumbents |
| 5 | E2 — Zone-level alert priority (vault vs. gallery) | **13** | S1, S2 | Configuration differentiator vs. basic IoT |
| 6 | E16 — Digital audit log, insurer/funder ready | **13** | S1, S3, S5 | Compliance automation removes manual admin |
| 7 | E19 — Compliance deficiency avoidance | **13** | All | Regulatory hygiene — covers grant-funding risk |
| 8 | E1 — No uncovered vault exits | **12** | S1, S2 | Pre-deployment coverage planning tool |

**Top opportunity cluster:** After-hours remote visibility (E8 + E11 + E12 + E18) — four of the top five needs are directly addressed by the Türwächter's IoT dashboard during closed-hours operation. E8 (vault activation, OS=17) is the highest-scoring individual need across the museum portfolio — higher than any equivalent in the hotels market — driven by the theft-prevention dimension absent from hospitality.

**Heritage advantage:** Constraint C5 (surface mount) inverts in this market — it is the **qualifying criterion**, not a friction point. Wired A2 alternative is disqualified in listed buildings without planning consent, clearing the Türwächter's primary competitor from the S2 segment entirely.

---

sections.title: Constraint Impact on Segments
sections.content:
| Constraint | S1 Large art/nat. hist. | S2 Historic house (listed) | S3 Regional / community | S4 Archaeological site | S5 Multi-site group |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C3 Wireless range | **Friction** (large buildings) | minor (compact) | none | **Primary risk** (dispersed) | mitigable |
| C4 Battery life | minor | none | **Friction** (infrequent maintenance) | **Friction** (remote access) | mitigable |
| C5 Surface mount | **Advantage** (no heritage consent needed) | **Advantage** (heritage requirement) | Advantage | none | Advantage |
| C6 EasyWave protocol | **Friction** (BMS in scope) | none | none | none | **Friction** (central BMS) |
| C7 Price premium | none | none | **Barrier** (tight budget) | **Barrier** | mitigable at portfolio scale |
| C8 GDPR | required (EU public body) | required | required | minor | **Required** (portfolio) |

**No knockout constraints.** C5 uniquely inverts — in heritage buildings it is the most important selection criterion in favour of the product.

---

sections.title: QA Self-Check
sections.content:
| # | Check | Status |
|---|---|---|
| V1 | All statements start with "Minimize the likelihood of…" or "Increase the ability to…" with exactly one KPI | PASS |
| V2 | product_related=true ONLY where need disappears with a different technology (E3, E4, E5, E13, E17) | PASS |
| V3 | No unrealistic scoring patterns; E8/E9 at imp=10 for safety/regulatory — appropriate | PASS |
| V4 | Impact categories match KPIs throughout | PASS |
| V5 | ≥2 needs per relevant job step; 26% product-related, 74% market-inherent | PASS |
| V6 | Zero references to "Türwächter", "EasyWave", "GfS", or "868 MHz" in need statements | PASS |
| V7 | Anchor = L5 (standalone product/system) — correct level | PASS |
| V8 | Product job statement includes parent VN unit ("Building Security & Life Safety") and context qualifier | PASS |
| — | Heritage constraint inversion documented (C5 = advantage, not friction) | PASS |
| — | Theft-prevention dimension correctly elevates E8 importance vs. hotels equivalent | PASS |

rows.CFJ: Acquire, preserve, and present cultural, historical, natural, and scientific heritage to the public through curated exhibitions, conservation, and educational programming

rows.Defining Circumstance: >200K visitors/yr; professional security team; high-value portable collection; BMS likely present
rows.Why distinct: Primary theft prevention concern; highest consequence per undetected exit event; IoT dashboard replaces costly guard post

rows.Defining Circumstance: Protected structure; cannot route wiring without heritage consent; minimal permanent staff
rows.Why distinct: C5 (surface mount) is an **advantage**, not a friction; battery-powered wireless is the only viable retrofit option

rows.Defining Circumstance: Small paid staff + volunteers; limited technical capacity; tight budget
rows.Why distinct: IoT price premium (C7) is a barrier; ROI must be simple and documentable; commissioning complexity is a decision killer

rows.Defining Circumstance: Dispersed perimeter; intermittent public access seasons; remote or semi-remote locations
rows.Why distinct: Range constraint (C3) is primary; battery life (C4) critical where maintenance visits are infrequent; no BMS

rows.Defining Circumstance: Manages portfolio of buildings under one security governance structure
rows.Why distinct: Cross-site IoT dashboard is the decisive value proposition; protocol lock-in (C6) relevant if centralized BMS is in scope

rows.Job Family: Protect collections, staff, and visitors against theft, unauthorized access, fire, and emergency exit misuse at all times
rows.Türwächter Relevance: **DIRECT ANCHOR** — product enters at L5 Emergency Exit Security Devices

rows.Job Family: Maintain building fabric, utilities, and physical infrastructure in operational and compliant condition
rows.Türwächter Relevance: Adjacent — heritage maintenance constraints amplify non-invasive installation requirement

rows.Job Family: Stabilize, treat, and store collection objects to prevent deterioration
rows.Türwächter Relevance: Adjacent — conservation storage areas are highest-risk exit points (direct collection access)

rows.Value: PROCESS_VN — product is used by the museum operator; it does not become part of the cultural output
Alarmed exit hardware with IoT monitoring (~20% market share in museum sector)

C5 (surface mount) = **advantage** in heritage buildings; C3 (range) = primary risk in large sites; C4 (battery) = critical at low-maintenance sites; C8 (GDPR) = EU public buildings; C6 (EasyWave protocol lock-in) = relevant for S1/S5 with BMS

rows.Statement: Acquire, preserve, and present cultural, historical, natural, and scientific heritage to the public through curated exhibitions, conservation, and educational programming

Help Building Security & Life Safety to detect and deter unauthorized use of emergency exit doors in collection spaces and storage areas while maintaining fire-code-compliant escape capability in heritage buildings where structural modification is prohibited or restricted

rows.Alternative: Basic mechanical door alarm (no monitoring) — 45% share
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: No remote visibility; requires physical patrol to detect unreported activation; no audit log; no battery required for alarm

rows.Alternative: Wired intruder detection + door contacts (alarm panel) — 25% share
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Wiring through heritage fabric requires planning consent and conservation officer approval — major barrier in listed buildings; high retrofit cost

rows.Alternative: **Alarmed exit hardware with IoT monitoring** (Türwächter) — 20% share
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Proprietary wireless protocol (C6); battery dependency (C4); ~730 EUR/door (C7); surface-mount profile is **accepted** in heritage context

High recurring labor cost (security = ~22% of museum operating budget); [Source: [Museum Running Costs](https://financialmodelslab.com/blogs/operating-costs/museum)] coverage gaps when attendant is elsewhere; no event log; scales linearly with door count

rows.Alternative: No dedicated exit monitoring (do nothing) — 10% share
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Zero installation burden; zero P1 burden; compliance risk is distributed across insurance, fire marshal, and grant-funding conditions

rows.Scenario: After-hours unauthorized activation of exit from collections storage vault or conservation area (possible theft attempt)
Visitor pushing through "emergency exit only" gallery door during opening hours (access to restricted area or shortcut)
Contractor/maintenance worker propping exit door during infrastructure work in a gallery or plant room
Multi-site museum group: centralized security monitoring across geographically distributed buildings with minimal staff at each
Heritage authority / fire marshal / insurer inspection — documentation of exit monitoring compliance required
Historic building retrofit: wired security system is impractical; battery-powered surface-mount device is the only consented option

rows.Notes: Monitors dashboard, responds to activations, investigates incidents
Accountable for theft prevention, compliance KPIs, insurer requirements
Defines specification; must obtain heritage consent for any structural work
Surface-mount installation; gateway pairing; heritage-compliant fixing
Signs off on fire safety compliance and conservation reversibility
Battery replacement; signal level checks; remote dashboard health

rows.Person in 712110: Insurer; heritage funder (Arts Council, Historic England); fire authority
rows.Notes: Sets compliance and reversibility requirements; may co-fund if aligned with heritage protection goals

rows.A2 Wired alarm panel: Engage integrator; heritage consent application; wiring survey; planning permission
rows.A3 IoT monitoring (Türwächter): Specify device+gateway; RF coverage survey; assess battery life for door frequency; certify conservation reversibility
rows.A4 Guard / attendant: Issue RFP or hire in-house; schedule SIA-licensed staff; SLA negotiation

rows.A1 Basic mech. alarm: Surface-mount per door; key provisioning; no wiring
rows.A2 Wired alarm panel: Obtain listed building consent; route conduit; install panel; programme zones
rows.A3 IoT monitoring (Türwächter): Surface-mount device; pair to gateway; configure zones; test alarm + radio; verify no permanent fixings required by heritage officer
rows.A4 Guard / attendant: Define patrol schedules and coverage map; brief staff; configure incident log

rows.A1 Basic mech. alarm: Manual patrol to verify no propping; respond only if alarm is heard
rows.A2 Wired alarm panel: Alarm panel event response; physical investigation per alert
rows.A3 IoT monitoring (Türwächter): Dashboard monitoring; alert triage by priority zone (storage vault vs. public gallery); dispatch investigation
rows.A4 Guard / attendant: Attendant presence in gallery; rounds outside hours; telephone incident report
rows.A5 Do nothing: Accept coverage gap; rely on CCTV retrospectively

rows.A1 Basic mech. alarm: Periodic tamper check; no battery required
rows.A2 Wired alarm panel: Annual panel service; wiring inspection; heritage-impacting maintenance needs re-consent
rows.A3 IoT monitoring (Türwächter): Battery replacement (~every 2 yrs); firmware update; signal level check; conservation environment check (temperature/humidity impact on battery)
rows.A4 Guard / attendant: Staff turnover; re-training; rota management; fatigue and coverage gap management

rows.A1 Basic mech. alarm: Remove hardware; fill screw holes in historic masonry
rows.A2 Wired alarm panel: Remove hardware; remove conduit; make-good — heritage restoration required
rows.A3 IoT monitoring (Türwächter): Remove device; decommission from dashboard; no permanent mark on historic fabric

rows.Statement (museum context): Determine which emergency exits require monitoring (storage vaults, conservation labs, galleries, public exits), alert priority by zone, and notification escalation rules
rows.Relevant to product: YES — zone configuration is critical; vault exits > public gallery exits

rows.Statement (museum context): Map all emergency exit points across building; assess wireless signal paths through historic masonry; identify gateway placement compatible with heritage constraints
rows.Relevant to product: YES — RF penetration through thick stone/brick walls is a key variable

rows.Statement (museum context): Install monitoring devices on door handles without drilling into historic fabric; pair to gateway; configure zone priorities; test alarm tone audibility
rows.Relevant to product: YES — surface-mount, no-drill installation is the primary market differentiator

rows.Statement (museum context): Verify every device transmits through heritage-grade masonry walls, alarm is unobstructed, escape motion is unrestricted, and heritage officer confirms reversibility
rows.Relevant to product: YES — acceptance testing must include RF validation through actual wall materials

rows.Statement (museum context): Maintain all monitored exits in secured state 24/7; trigger alert on unauthorized activation; distinguish high-risk zones (storage) from lower-risk zones (public exits)
rows.Relevant to product: YES — core function; after-hours monitoring is the primary use case

rows.Statement (museum context): Track real-time exit status from centralized dashboard; receive alerts prioritized by zone risk level; correlate with CCTV on high-risk events
rows.Relevant to product: YES — primary IoT value; after-hours dashboard access on mobile is essential for lean security teams

rows.Statement (museum context): Respond to and reset triggered devices; adjust zone alert rules; replace failed units with minimal disruption to gallery operations or open days
rows.Relevant to product: YES — operational; reset must not require tool or key unavailable on night shift

rows.Statement (museum context): Generate compliance audit reports for fire marshal, insurer, and heritage authority; proactively replace batteries before monitoring capability is lost
rows.Relevant to product: YES — compliance documentation is a grant-funding and insurance requirement for many institutions

rows.Full Statement: Minimize the likelihood of high-value collection storage area exits remaining unmonitored due to incomplete upfront coverage planning, leaving ≥1 after-hours exit event undetected per quarter

Increase the ability to segment emergency exit monitoring alerts by zone risk level (storage vault vs. public gallery vs. service corridor) so that a single after-hours activation of a vault exit triggers immediate escalation
Increase the ability to pre-validate wireless signal coverage through heritage-grade masonry walls (stone, brick, reinforced concrete) before committing to gateway placement and device purchase
Minimize the likelihood of RF signal loss between exit devices and the gateway caused by thick historic masonry walls, creating silent monitoring gaps exceeding 30 minutes
Minimize the time required to install and commission emergency exit monitoring across a 20-room museum without drilling into historic fabric — achieving full coverage in <1 day
Minimize the likelihood of installation fixing methods leaving permanent marks or penetrations in listed building fabric, causing non-compliance with heritage conservation officer requirements

Increase the ability to verify that every installed exit device transmits correctly through actual building materials and that alarm operation is unimpeded — within a single commissioning walk-through that the heritage officer can witness
Minimize the likelihood of an unauthorized emergency exit activation in a storage vault or conservation area remaining undetected for >2 minutes during closed hours
Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit monitoring device adding any resistance or additional step to the escape path that could delay evacuation of visitors with reduced mobility

Minimize the likelihood of legitimate authorized uses (staff exiting at end of shift, conservation team accessing storage) generating false alerts that erode duty manager confidence in the monitoring system

Increase the ability of a single duty security officer or remote monitoring team to track real-time exit status across all monitored doors from one centralized interface without physical patrol — during closed-hours coverage windows
Minimize the time from emergency exit activation event to security officer notification to <60 seconds during after-hours monitoring periods
Minimize the likelihood of wireless connectivity loss to any monitored exit device creating a silent gap exceeding 15 minutes — particularly during after-hours periods when no staff are on-site

Minimize the time to reset a triggered exit monitoring device and restore it to active monitoring state after an incident investigation — without requiring specialist tools not available to on-duty museum staff
Minimize the likelihood of a high-priority storage area activation being deprioritized due to alert fatigue from higher-frequency low-risk public gallery exit events

Increase the ability to produce a complete, timestamped digital log of all emergency exit activations and response actions formatted for submission to the fire authority, insurer, and heritage funder — without manual compilation

Minimize the time required to identify and proactively replace batteries approaching depletion threshold across a deployment of ≥10 exit monitoring devices before monitoring capability is lost — accounting for reduced battery performance in climate-controlled conservation environments

Minimize the variability in emergency exit monitoring coverage status across the building at any time — ensuring no exits enter an unmonitored state due to technical failure without triggering a staff notification

Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit compliance deficiencies being cited during fire marshal inspection or heritage authority review — resulting in temporary closure of gallery spaces or loss of grant-funding eligibility

rows.Retrieval: "Becomes the person who prevented the theft — or who can prove it was investigated within minutes"
rows.Bridge: "What must the system do so they CAN demonstrate rapid, documented response to any after-hours exit event?"
rows.Market Need: Increase the ability to demonstrate evidence-based, timestamped response to after-hours exit activations — to insurers and collections trustees — without manual log reconstruction [hypothesis]

rows.Retrieval: "The board, insurers, and heritage funders must see: this institution takes collection protection seriously and uses state-of-the-art monitoring compatible with its building's heritage status"
rows.Bridge: "What must the system do so funders and auditors SEE a professional, non-invasive protection approach?"
rows.Market Need: Minimize the likelihood of a preventable exit monitoring failure becoming a documented finding in an insurer audit or heritage authority inspection that affects funding eligibility [hypothesis]

rows.Retrieval: "Wants to feel they would know immediately if someone was in the building after hours; fears being the person who didn't notice until a piece went missing"
rows.Bridge: "What must the system do to produce confidence in continuous after-hours coverage with minimal staff?"
rows.Market Need: Minimize the sense of uncertainty about whether high-risk storage and collection exits are truly secured during closed-hours periods when no patrol is possible [hypothesis]

rows.Error Statement (short): E8 — Vault exit activation undetected >2 min after hours
rows.Action: Core differentiation against A1 and A4

rows.Error Statement (short): E11 — Single officer covers all exits, no patrol
rows.Action: IoT dashboard is the primary labour-cost ROI story

rows.Error Statement (short): E12 — Alert latency <60 sec after hours
E18 — Monitoring consistency, no silent gaps
E2 — Zone-level alert priority (vault vs. gallery)
E16 — Digital audit log, insurer/funder ready

rows.Check: All statements start with "Minimize the likelihood of…" or "Increase the ability to…" with exactly one KPI
product_related=true ONLY where need disappears with a different technology (E3, E4, E5, E13, E17)
No unrealistic scoring patterns; E8/E9 at imp=10 for safety/regulatory — appropriate
≥2 needs per relevant job step; 26% product-related, 74% market-inherent
Zero references to "Türwächter", "EasyWave", "GfS", or "868 MHz" in need statements
Product job statement includes parent VN unit ("Building Security & Life Safety") and context qualifier
Heritage constraint inversion documented (C5 = advantage, not friction)
Theft-prevention dimension correctly elevates E8 importance vs. hotels equivalent

jtbd.market_name: Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions
jtbd.product: GfS Türwächter / Exit Control 179/1125 with IoT

job_map.product_job_statement: Help Building Security & Life Safety to detect and deter unauthorized use of emergency exit doors in collection spaces and storage areas while maintaining fire-code-compliant escape capability in heritage buildings where structural modification is prohibited or restricted

job_steps.relevance_rationale: Zone configuration by collection risk level; vault exits require higher-priority alert rules than public gallery exits
RF coverage planning through heritage-grade masonry; gateway placement subject to conservation constraints
Surface-mount installation without drilling; heritage officer sign-off on reversibility
Acceptance test of radio transmission through actual wall materials; alarm audibility and escape motion verification
24/7 exit security with heightened after-hours theft-prevention priority
After-hours dashboard — primary IoT value for lean or remote security teams
Alert zone rule adjustment; device reset by duty staff without specialist tools
Compliance documentation for fire marshal, insurer, heritage funder; battery health management in conservation environments

Alarmed exit hardware with IoT monitoring (Türwächter)
